reasons: Fast ranged play without asking you to learn a full caster rotation.
Clear skill jobs: Fragmentation Rounds for packs, High Velocity Rounds for rares and bosses, Escape Shot for safety.
Good first-route choice if you want weapon feel but still need readable campaign priorities.
The build stays practical as long as you do not add grenade layers too early.

explanation.playstyleFit: This fits fast or ranged players because the crossbow loop rewards spacing, movement, and quick target swaps.
explanation.difficultyFit: It is beginner-readable, but not fully hands-off. You still need to keep the crossbow upgraded and use Escape Shot before enemies close in.
explanation.learningCurve: Learn one clear shot, one boss shot, and one escape tool first. Add grenades only after that loop feels stable.
explanation.strengths: Strong early identity, clear skill roles, safe range, and direct links into Mercenary crossbow pages.
explanation.weaknesses: Reload rhythm and tool swapping can feel busy if you wanted the simplest possible first character.

reasons: Best fit when durability matters more than campaign speed.
Simple early priorities: slam for packs, direct hit for tougher enemies, defenses before luxury damage.
Forgives more positioning mistakes than fragile ranged or caster starters.
Good path for complete beginners who want fewer combat decisions.

explanation.playstyleFit: Tanky players usually want time to read fights. Warrior gives that through heavier hits, armor priorities, and slower deliberate pacing.
explanation.difficultyFit: This is one of the safer beginner recommendations because the build does not depend on fast combo timing.
explanation.learningCurve: The curve is mostly about weapon upgrades, using slam skills before packs surround you, and learning boss tells without panic movement.
explanation.strengths: Durability, simple gearing priorities, readable melee identity, and strong beginner patience.
explanation.weaknesses: It will not feel as fast as Monk, Ranger, or Mercenary, and slow attacks punish weak weapons.

reasons: Minions create space while you learn movement, bosses, and flask timing.
The first version is easy to understand: keep minions alive, stay safe, add one support button.
Better for cautious players than active melee or reload-heavy weapon routes.
The build has direct skill links for Skeletal Warrior, Skeletal Sniper, and Raise Spectre.

explanation.playstyleFit: Summoner players want allies to absorb pressure. Minion Witch is the clearest route for that job.
explanation.difficultyFit: It is a strong complete-beginner fit because mistakes are less punishing when minions are holding attention.
explanation.learningCurve: The main learning curve is minion uptime and positioning. You can learn the campaign without mastering a fast damage rotation first.
explanation.strengths: Safe pacing, beginner-friendly fight reading, low melee risk, and a clean class identity.
explanation.weaknesses: It is weaker for players who want direct weapon impact or dislike managing minion state.

reasons: Good caster route when you want spell damage but do not want a huge rotation.
Flame Wall teaches setup casting without turning the whole build into a planner problem.
Works well as a support layer for Spark, Fireball, or other simple spell packages.
Best when you are comfortable placing damage before tougher enemies reach you.

explanation.playstyleFit: Spellcaster players get a clear caster decision: use Flame Wall as the support layer, then decide whether Spark or Fireball is carrying the route.
explanation.difficultyFit: It fits beginners who want spells, but it asks for a little setup timing. It is not as passive as Minion Witch.
explanation.learningCurve: Learn when to place Flame Wall before rares and bosses. Do not add several setup skills before your main clear spell feels stable.
explanation.strengths: Readable caster identity, strong support-skill logic, and useful links into Flame Wall and caster build pages.
explanation.weaknesses: It can feel clunky if you wanted one-button clearing or if movement and defenses are still weak.

reasons: Best fit when you want active melee speed instead of slow tank pacing.
Monk has clear payoff skills: Falling Thunder for identity, Tempest Bell for tougher enemies, Killing Palm for finishers.
Rewards movement and timing, which suits players with some ARPG experience.
Keeps the first route focused instead of mixing random melee and ranged ideas.

explanation.playstyleFit: Fast melee players usually want movement and timing. Monk fits that better than Warrior.
explanation.difficultyFit: This is better for players with some ARPG experience because the build asks you to move, time hits, and avoid trading too often.
explanation.learningCurve: Commit to one Monk damage identity first, then add Tempest Bell when single-target payoff becomes the issue.
explanation.strengths: High tempo, strong class fantasy, clear skill progression, and good internal links into Monk pages.
explanation.weaknesses: Not ideal for low-input play, face-tanking, or players who want minions to handle pressure.

reasons: Good ranged choice if you want familiar bow clear instead of crossbow rhythm.
Simple skill package: one bow clear tool, one escape answer, and steady weapon upgrades.
Better for players who value distance and movement over durability.
Cleaner than hybrid routes if you already know you want bows.

explanation.playstyleFit: Ranged players who prefer bows over crossbows get a more familiar campaign rhythm here.
explanation.difficultyFit: The route is easy to understand, but it still asks for movement because Ranger is not built to stand still.
explanation.learningCurve: Keep Lightning Arrow as the clear identity and upgrade bow damage before adding secondary packages.
explanation.strengths: Fast clear, familiar ranged gameplay, and simple route ownership.
explanation.weaknesses: Less forgiving than Warrior and less protected than Minion Witch when positioning goes wrong.

reasons: Only recommended when you already accept hybrid uncertainty.
Good for players who want to experiment instead of following the safest documented route.
Works best when you choose melee-first or spell-first early instead of splitting gear priorities.
Useful fallback for experienced players who do not care about complexity.

explanation.playstyleFit: Druid fits players who want hybrid melee and spell decisions instead of a narrow first-character route.
explanation.difficultyFit: This is not a complete-beginner recommendation. It belongs to experienced players who can adapt as guidance changes.
explanation.learningCurve: The hard part is choosing one early identity and not spreading upgrades across every possible tool.
explanation.strengths: Flexible fantasy, room to experiment, and a strong fit for players who enjoy testing.
explanation.weaknesses: Less documented, less direct, and easier to make messy than the safer starter routes.
